FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzed prospective cohort data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study (n = 9,208), U.S. national sample, with baseline screen time exposure and 2-year follow-up OCD outcome.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: logistic regression on prospective cohort data examining associations between baseline self-reported screen time and OCD diagnosis at follow-up, excluding participants with baseline OCD.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in The Journal of Adolescent Health (2023); no evidence of duplicate or preprint.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 succeeds: phone/SM-specific variables paired with individual-trait outcome. Main finding: "Each additional hour of total screen time was prospectively associated with 1.05 higher odds of OCD"; modality-specific estimates for "playing video games" (aOR 1.15) and "watching videos" (aOR 1.11) also paired with OCD (individual-trait behavioral/health outcome). Other side is individual-trait-level (OCD diagnosis, a mental health condition).

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: OR 1.05 (95% CI 1.01-1.09) for total screen time; aOR 1.15 (95% CI 1.03-1.28) for video games; aOR 1.11 (95% CI 1.01-1.23) for watching videos.

FT_IC4.evidence: All quantitative estimates reported with 95% confidence intervals; logistic regression adjusted for race/ethnicity, sex, household income, parent education, family history, and study site.

FT_EC7_reason: Prospective design with SM as primary IV (exposure), not as sole DV across all analyses. FT-EC7 exclusion does not apply.
notes: All three screen time modalities (total, video games, watching videos) meet FT-IC2 criteria. Paper clearly distinguishes platform modalities, meeting FT-IC2 specificity requirement.

FT_IC1.evidence: Cross-sectional data from Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (n = 10,280), Year 2, 2018-2020, U.S. national sample of early adolescents aged 10-14.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: regression analyses examining associations between self-reported bedtime screen use and self- and caregiver-reported sleep measures in early adolescents.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Sleep Health (2023); no evidence of duplicate or preprint.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 succeeds: multiple phone/SM-specific variables paired with individual-trait sleep outcome. Examples: "television or Internet-connected electronic device in the bedroom" (aRR 1.27, 1.15 for sleep disturbance); "left their phone ringer activated overnight" (associated with trouble falling/staying asleep and sleep disturbance); "streaming movies, playing video games, listening to music, talking/texting on the phone, and using social media or chat rooms were all associated with trouble falling/staying asleep." Other side is individual-trait-level (sleep disturbance symptoms, a health/behavioral outcome).

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results: aRR 1.27 (95% CI 1.12-1.44) for TV/Internet device in bedroom and trouble falling/staying asleep; aRR 1.15 (95% CI 1.06-1.25) for overall sleep disturbance; modality-specific associations with streaming, gaming, music, texting, and social media.

FT_IC4.evidence: All quantitative estimates reported as adjusted risk ratios (aRR) with 95% confidence intervals; regression adjusted for sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parent education, depression, data collection period, and study site.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design (binding carve-out: if any cross-sectional analysis with SM as DV exists, FT-EC7 does not apply). FT-EC7 exclusion criterion requires all analyses to be longitudinal; this study's cross-sectional design exempts it from FT-EC7.
notes: Multiple bedtime screen use behaviors assessed, including device presence, phone activation, and specific modalities (streaming, gaming, texting, social media). All paired with sleep outcomes (individual-trait-level).

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzed data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study (N = 11,875), U.S. national cohort of 9-11-year-old children, with baseline screen time and 1-year follow-up behavioral outcomes.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis: Modified Poisson regression examining associations between baseline child-reported screen time (total and by modality) and parent-reported conduct and oppositional defiant disorder at 1-year follow-up.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry (2023); no evidence of duplicate or preprint.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 succeeds: multiple phone/SM-specific variables paired with individual-trait outcomes. Modality-specific estimates: social media (PR 1.62, 95% CI 1.39-1.87 for conduct disorder); video chat (PR 1.21 for ODD); texting (PR 1.19 for ODD); television/movies (PR 1.17 for ODD); video games (PR 1.14 for ODD). All paired with individual-trait-level outcomes (conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder, both behavioral/psychiatric diagnoses).

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative estimates: total screen time PR 1.07 for conduct disorder, PR 1.05 for ODD; social media PR 1.62 (conduct); video chat PR 1.21 (ODD); texting PR 1.19 (ODD); TV/movies PR 1.17 (ODD); video games PR 1.14 (ODD). Threshold analysis: >4 hr/day associated with 69% higher prevalence of conduct disorder and 46% higher prevalence of ODD.

FT_IC4.evidence: All quantitative results reported as prevalence ratios (PR) with 95% confidence intervals; Modified Poisson regression adjusted for potential confounders including race/ethnicity, sex, household income, parent education, and other baseline characteristics.

FT_EC7_reason: Prospective design with multiple SM-as-IV analyses (social media, video chat, texting, TV/movies, video games all as exposures predicting behavioral outcomes). FT-EC7 exclusion requires (a) longitudinal design AND (b) SM as DV in every analysis AND (c) NO SM-as-IV anywhere; this paper has SM consistently as IV across modalities, so FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: Strongest association between social media and conduct disorder (PR 1.62). Paper explicitly distinguishes contemporary screen time modalities with separate quantitative estimates for each, meeting FT-IC2 specificity requirement.
